coredata.dc:title: In-situ incorporation of carbon dots into mesoporous nickel boride for regulating photocatalytic activities
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Xuejun
Chang, Qing
Li, Ning
Wang, Huiqi
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Mesoporous nanomaterials not only possess strong ability to adsorb and interact with guest species both on their outer and inner surfaces, but also exhibit nanoscale effects in their channels and on the walls of their pores. This opens up intriguing possibilities for the design of highly activity catalysts by means of mesoporous materials. Here we present a facile method for the in-situ incorporation of carbon dots into mesoporous nickel boride and their subsequent use as photcatalysts. In addition to promoting photogenerated charge separation and transfer in the resultant system, carbon dots can also alter the band edge positions and surface electronegativity of nickel boride, thus significantly enhancing the processes involved in heterogeneous catalytic reaction. Our prepared samples exhibit remarkably enhanced activity for catalytic conversion of nitrobenzene derivatives under visible light irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mesoporous
Photocatalysis
Band edge positions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125 , 51272301 , 51502270 ), Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province , Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi , North University of China Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars , and North University of China Fund for Scientific Innovation Team . We also thank our collaborator Dr Adrian Trinchi of the CSIRO Australia for his scientific discussions on this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of NiB (a-c), the insets in (c) is the corresponding fast Fourier transform image; TEM and HRTEM images of CDs@NiB (d-f), the insets in (f) is the fast Fourier transform image of the nanoparticle labeled by white circle; (g) HRTEM images of CDs; (h) EDS line scanning profiles along the line in the inset for a nanosphere of CDs@NiB. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XRD patterns of S1 and S2; (b) Pore size distributions of S1 and S2; (c) XPS C 1s spectrum of S1; (d) XPS C 1s spectrum of S2; (e) XPS B 1s spectra of S1 and S2; (f) XPS Ni 2p spectra of S1 and S2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic of in-situ incorporation of CDs into amorphous and mesoporous NiB nanospheres. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Comparisons of UV-vis light absorption from S1 and S2; (b) Absorbance spectra of the 4-NP solution with NaBH4 in the presence of suspended S1 before and after visible light irradiation of 3 min; (c) A series of absorbance spectra of the 4-NP solution with NaBH4 in the presence of suspended S2 in various time of visible light irradiation; (d and e) A series of absorbance spectra of the 4-NA solution with NaBH4 in the presence of suspended S1 and S2, respectively, in various time of visible light irradiation; (f) The comparisons of conversion rate from 4-NP/4-NA to 4-AP/4-AA using S1 and S2 as a photocatalyst under the given irradiation time, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The effect of CD content in samples on photoreduction activity. (a) Absorbance spectra of the 4-NP solution with NaBH4 in the presence of suspended samples (S1-S4) containing different amounts of CDs after visible light irradiation of 3 min; (b) Conversion rates from 4-NP to 4-AP using S1-S4 as photocatalysts for 3 min irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Cathodic scan for determining the CBM of S1 and S2; (b) Anodic scan for determining the VBM of S1 and S2; (c) EIS Nyquist plots of S1 and S2; (d) Transient photocurrent responses of the S1 and S2; (e) Schematic energy level diagrams of S1 and S2 in comparison with the potential for 4-NP reduction; (f) Zeta potential values of S1, S2, 4-NP and 4-AP, in which 1 mg samples were dispersed in 2 mL DI water by ultrasonic. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration for the preparation of S1 and S2.

head.ce:title: In-situ incorporation of carbon dots into mesoporous nickel boride for regulating photocatalytic activities

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mesoporous nanomaterials not only possess strong ability to adsorb and interact with guest species both on their outer and inner surfaces, but also exhibit nanoscale effects in their channels and on the walls of their pores. This opens up intriguing possibilities for the design of highly activity catalysts by means of mesoporous materials. Here we present a facile method for the in-situ incorporation of carbon dots into mesoporous nickel boride and their subsequent use as photcatalysts. In addition to promoting photogenerated charge separation and transfer in the resultant system, carbon dots can also alter the band edge positions and surface electronegativity of nickel boride, thus significantly enhancing the processes involved in heterogeneous catalytic reaction. Our prepared samples exhibit remarkably enhanced activity for catalytic conversion of nitrobenzene derivatives under visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the significant challenges in chemical catalysis is the development of approaches that enable both control and manipulation of the activity, selectivity, and specificity of the catalysts [1]. It has been shown that large specific surface areas can contribute significantly to catalytic efficiency [1–3]. Nanomaterials typically have significantly larger surface areas than their micro-sized counterparts, in addition to enhanced reactivities, and these attributes can result in improved catalysts [1,3]. In contrast to solid nanoparticles, hollow nanostructures have much greater surface areas as well as offering several unique properties [1,2]. For instance, the activity and selectivity of nanocatalysts can be dramatically increased by tailoring their pore size. Moreover, the immobilization of catalysts in both nano-confined spaces as well as their exposed surfaces can significantly improve their activity and selectivity [4–6]. Bao et al. demonstrated that the catalytic efficiency of Rh nanoparticles was strikingly increased for the conversion of CO and H2 to ethanol while they were confined inside carbon nanotubes [7]. The overall rate of ethanol production inside carbon nanotubes exceeded that on the outside of carbon nanotubes by more than an order of magnitude, even though the latter provided much more accessible spaces for the catalytic reaction [7]. This kind of synergetic confinement creates an avenue to manipulate the activities and selectivities of catalytic systems through mesoporous nanostructured materials.

Carbon dots (CDs) refer to monodisperse carbonaceous nanoparticles with less than 10 nm in size and possess tunable photoluminescence, unique photo-induced charge transfer, and electron reservoir properties [8–11]. When compare to the more traditional semiconductor quantum dots, CDs present many competitive advantages, such as ease of synthesis, low cost, excellent stability, non-toxicity and high biocompatibility [9,10]. Accordingly, the CDs have increasing become scaffolds for applications in photocatalytic solar-to-energy conversion besides bioimaging, sensors and drug delivery [9–11]. Recently, a series of CD-based photocatalysts, including CDs coupled with nanostructured semiconductor, conjugated compounds, and noble metals, have been developed for photocatalytic degradation, H2 evolution, CO2 conversion, or fine organic synthesis [12–19]. However, most of these reports presented the heterostructures of CD-covered outer surface of solid nanostructured materials. The few experimental examples of porous heterostructures containing CDs had far larger pore sizes than CDs [20–23]. In addition, their preparations usually include two steps: In the first place CDs and their counterparts are synthesized, respectively and subsequently combined by physical adsorption. It is hence absolutely vital to develop suitable in-situ incorporation methods to incorporate CDs into much smaller nanochannels for understanding the effects of nano-confined spaces on the activities of CD-based catalysts.

Herein, we provide a facile route for the in-situ incorporation of CDs into mesoporous, amorphous nickel boride nanospheres (CDs@NiB). The as-prepared nanohybrids have nanochannels with several nanometers in diameter, and they exhibit remarkable ability for the photoreduction of nitrobenzene derivatives. As revealed by electrochemical characterization, in-situ incorporation of CDs in CDs@NiB not only significantly improved the charge-transfer properties through decreasing interface impedance, but also modulates the energy band levels of nickel boride to align with the reduction potential of nitrobenzene derivatives.

Preparation of CDs@NiB (sample S1): Preparing processes are shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, a piece of Ni foam (NF, 1 cm2, 50 mg) was cleaned ultrasonically with acetone and again with 3 M HCl solution for 10 min each time, and subsequently washed with DI water several times. Secondly, the cleaned NF was submerged into a beaker containing 20 mL of an aqueous 0.5 M citric acid solution and reacted at 80 °C until it was completely dissolved while stirring. Thirdly, the resulting solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and then maintained at 200 °C for 5 h. Thus, the solution of CDs with Ni2+ ions was obtained and named as CDs@Ni2+ solution. Fourthly, after adjusting its pH to a value between 9 and 11, solid NaBH4 powder was added to this solution with Ni2+ ions on the basis of molar ratio of Ni:B = 1:4 (i.e. 129 mg NaBH4) and then stirred for 3 h at room temperature. Finally, the black precipitate obtained by centrifugation was thoroughly washed with distilled water and ethanol several times and dried in vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h. Thus, the S1 was obtained.
Preparation of NiB (sample S2): The S2 was prepared following almost the same recipe and process as for S1 but without containing third step, i.e. the formation procedure of CDs@Ni2+ solution.

Preparation of samples S3 and S4: The S3 and S4 were prepared following the same recipe and process as for S1 but the concentration of CDs@Ni2+ solution decreased to one-quarter and half of that in S1, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution TEM and energy-dispersive spectra (EDS) were performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. TEM samples were prepared by dropping dilute products onto carbon coated copper grids. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of different samples were obtained using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE powder diffractometer at CuKα with a scanning rate of 2° min−1 in the 2θ range of 5–90°. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were determined at 77 K using a Micromeritics ASAP2020 surface area and porosity analyzer. Light absorption spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV-vis spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of all samples were collected using a Kratos AXIS 165 mutitechnique electron spectrometer with an Al Ka X-ray source for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. Zeta potential of samples was monitored using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS analyser.

Electrochemical measurements were performed in the three-electrode electrochemical cell system. 2 mg of sample was dispersed in 1 mL ethanol absolute by ultrasonic vibrations and then the sample suspension was dropped onto 1 cm × 2 cm indium tin oxide (ITO) glass electrodes. After dried in a vacuum at 70 °C, sample electrodes were obtained and sample thicknesses onto ITO were about 1 μm. 0.5 M Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte solution, and a Pt coil and Ag/AgCl reference electrode acted as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. Linear potential scans were performed at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 within a given potential window. All of the data were collected on a Bio-logic (SP 120, France) electrochemical workstation.

Typically, 2 mg of solid sample powder was added in an aqueous reaction solution obtained by mixing 10 mL of 0.05 mM 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), and 10 mL of 10 mM NaBH4. Herein, NaBH4 behaves as a reductant, but it is incapable of reducing 4-NP to the corresponding amino compound (4-Aminophenol, 4-AP) in the absence of any catalyst. The solution was irradiated with light from a 300 W xenon lamp placed 30 cm away. Optical cut-off filters were utilized to remove wavelengths below 420 nm, ensuring only visible and IR light impinged the samples during the photocatalytic tests. All photocatalytic reactions were conducted in a room with tightly controlled air conditioning in order to minimize effects from ambient heating. The light intensity reaching the solution was about 100 mW cm−2. The decrease in 4-nitrophenol concentration and the formation of reduction products were monitored using UV−vis spectrophotometry by taking aliquots at regular intervals.

It is well-known that amorphous NiB alloy can be produced by chemical reduction of nickel salts [24–27]. However, previous reports mostly presented solid nanoparticles of NiB without regular morphololgies [24–27]. As shown in the TEM images (Fig. 1 a), our prepared NiB (i.e. S2) exhibits a uniform sphere-like shape with mean sphere diameter ∼200 nm. In particular, the NiB nanosphere is a mesoporous structure with an abundance of nanochannels (Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image for a typical nanosphere displays no clear lattice fringes (Fig. 1c). Meanwhile, the fast Fourier transform image belonging to NiB amorphous nanosphere does not reveal any cleardiffraction spots (the inset of Fig. 1c), indicating that the NiB nanospheres possess a long-ranged disordered atomic alignment. In contrast, the nanospheres become larger in diameter when the CDs incorporate into NiB (S1, Fig. 1d), but the nanochannels in them seem like smaller than those in NiB nanospheres (Fig. 1e). HRTEM images reveal that within the S1, there are crystalline nanoparticles with a diameter in the range of 2–5 nm (Fig. 1f). Further fast Fourier transform patterns corresponding to the labeled nanoparticle show clear diffraction spots, proving good crystallinity of the nanoparticles. These crystalline features results from the CD nanoparticles.

Many previous works have demonstrated the formation of CDs using citric acid as carbon source under the hydrothermal condition given in this work [28–30]. We also provide photoluminescence spectra (Fig. S1, Supplementary information) and HRTEM images of as-prepared CDs (Fig. 1g and Fig. S2). The prepared CDs with 2–5 nm in diameter exhibit wavelength-dependent photoluminescence as reported [28–30], and crystalline features with lattices of about 0.31 nm, which is close to the (002) diffraction facets of graphite carbon. The observed shapes and structures (Fig. 1g) is identical to those of nanoparticles in the NiB, as shown in Fig. 1f. We further conduct EDS line-scanning analysis to investigate the elemental distribution. Fig. 1h illustrates that Ni and C elements distribute increasingly alone the line shown in the inset of Fig. 1h, and their intensities display obvious fluctuation. It is noted that most of positions of the Ni element decrease coincide with the positions of the C element increase. This should be attributed to the incorporation of small carbon nanoparticles (i.e. CDs) into NiB nanospheres.

XRD patterns of the S1 and S2 shown in Fig. 2 a both reveal a broad peak at 45° that is assigned to amorphous NiB based on previous reports (Fig. S3, Supplementary information) [24,25,27]. The XRD pattern of S1 presents another broad peak at 25°, which results from the CDs incorporated in NiB nanosphere. Channel-size distributions in nanospheres were determined by N2 adsorption-desorption analysis using Brunauer−Emmett−Teller (BET) method (Fig. S4, Supplementary information). The isotherms of the S1 and S2 display the hysteresis loop of type H3. This appearance demonstrates the presence of mesoporous architecture in S1 and S2. According to the Barrett−Joyner−Halenda equation, the pore size distributions of the S1 and S2 are calculated and shown in Fig. 2b. It can be seen that the diameter of nanochannels in S1 and S2 range from 2.7 to 10 nm. Furthermore, the incorporation of CDs into NiB leads to a reduction of the channel size within the nanosphere especially those in the range of 3–5 nm. These results are well in agreement with the above TEM observation.

The chemical composition of the S1 and S2 was then investigated using XPS. The XPS results indicate that both samples are mainly composed of Ni, B, C, and O elements (Fig. S5, Supplementary information). The contents of these elements are 12.565, 13.532, 43.412, 30.491 at.% in S1 while they are 17.409, 18.715, 29.784, 34.092 at.% in S2, respectively. The atom ratio of Ni:B is close to 1. What's more, the C content of S1 is much higher than that of S2, suggesting the formation of CDs@NiB. The valence states of S1 and S2 were identified by high-resolved XPS spectra. The C 1s spectra of samples S1 and S2 can be fitted into three peaks at around 284.7, 286.0 and 288.2 eV, which are attributed to sp2/sp3 C-C, C-O and C=O bonding, respectively (Fig. 2c and d) [19]. By contrast, the relative content of oxygen-containing groups in S1 is significantly less than that in S2. The CDs form as a result of the carbonization of citric acid and hence the citric acid content decreases in the reaction system [29,30]. The effects with less citric acid available during the reaction on the formation of NiB could give rise to the decrease of oxygen-containing groups in S1 in contrast with S2. As shown in Fig. 2e, the B exists mainly in an oxidized state [24]. Theoretically, the boron oxide is expected to be present due to a strong attraction of the anchored B for the oxygen [24,26]. This result closely corresponds to previous reports of NiB catalysts [24–27]. The intensity of B 1s in S1 is distinctly lower than that of S2, further confirming the incorporation of CDs into the NiB nanosphere. Fig. 2f depicts the Ni 2p core-level binding energy signals. The peaks at 855.6 and 873.4 eV are assigned to Ni (2p3/2) and Ni (2p1/2) of oxidized nickel, while the peaks at 852.3 and 870.0 eV are assigned to Ni (2p3/2) and Ni (2p1/2) of metallic nickel [24,27]. The above XPS results of B 1s and Ni 2p spectra are in well agreement with those of the amorphous NiB reported previously (Fig. S6, Supplementary information) [24,25,27]. Consequently, all of the characterizations performed support the formation of amorphous NiB and CDs-incorporated NiB via our presented preparation.

The incorporation of CDs into mesoporous NiB nanospheres could be induced by heterogeneous nucleation [17]. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the Ni2+ ions are firstly adsorbed on the surface of CDs in the reaction solution. Experimentally, zeta potential measurements indicate that the surface charges of CDs are −27.4 mV, favoring electrostatic interaction between CDs and positively charged Ni2+ ions. Upon addition of NaBH4 to the reaction solution, the Ni2+ ions on the surface of CDs are reduced which subsequently results in nucleation of NiB on CDs. Besides the nucleation process, the growth and aggregation processes are also very fast, thereby making the CDs involved in the formation of mesoporous NiB nanospheres. Finally, the nanospheres of CDs@NiB are formed and have a larger volume owing to the incorporation of CDs. To demonstrate above assumption, the sample morphological features of the intermediate process in fourth step were observed by TEM. From Fig. S7 (in Supplementary information), it can be seen that a great number of heterogeneous nanoparticles, which comprise of CDs with crystalline structures and amorphous NiB nanoparticles, are gradually assembled alone the axis of large nanospheres. Accordingly, TEM observations are just as schematically illustrated in Fig. 3.

The S1 exhibits broad light absorption in the visible light range (Fig. 4 a). After coupling with CDs, the absorption band of the S2 from UV to visible spectrum make significantly strong (Fig. 4a), suggesting that the addition of CDs effectively enhances the adsorption of NiB nanosphere. To estimate the light conversion efficiency of samples, the photoreduction of nitrobenzene derivatives were considered in our research. Taking 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) compound as an example, in practice NaBH4 is incapable of reducing 4-NP to the corresponding amino compound, namely 4-aminophenol (4-AP), and the typical absorption peak intensity of 4-NP remains unaffected with time [31–33]. By detecting the changes of the typical absorption peak intensity, the degree of the conversion reaction of 4-NP to 4-AP may be easily determined [32,33]. Pure 4-NP has a significant absorption peak at 315 nm, and this peak will shift to 400 nm (forming 4-nitrophenolate ions) when a freshly prepared NaBH4 solution is added to the 4-NP [31,32]. Therefore, the absorption peak at 400 nm becomes typical peak for tracking reduction process of 4-NP by UV−vis spectroscopy.

Fig. 4b shows the time-dependent absorption of 4-NP solution upon S1 in the presence of excess NaBH4. Under the visible light irradiation, the maximum absorption peak at 400 nm quickly decreases to almost zero in 3 min, accompanied by a concomitant increase of the peak at around 300 nm. This new absorption peak at 300 nm is attributed to the formation of 4-AP. Meanwhile, two isosbestic points are present at 277 and 318 nm, implying the clean conversion without yielding any byproducts. When the S2 acts as a photocatalyst, the absorption peak at 400 nm gradually declines as the reduction reaction time proceeds and the concomitant peak at 300 nm increases slowly (Fig. 4c). By comparison, the photocatalytic activity of S1 for reduction of 4-NP is remarkably higher than that of S2. Further confirm is given by the photoreduction of 4-nitroaniline (4-NA), which was examined in the presence of S1 and S2, respectively. Under the same pH condition, the typical absorption peak of 4-NA is located at around 380 nm while that of the corresponding reduzate, i.e. 4-Aminoaniline (4-AA), is around 305 nm. Under the visible light irradiation, the addition of S1 to a solution of 4-NA and NaBH4 results in a gradual decrease of the 380 nm band, and simultaneous appearance of a band centered at 305 nm (Fig. 4d) corresponding to the formation of the 4-AA. Although the photoreduction rate of 4-NA is slower than that of 4-NP, nearly complete conversion of 4-NA to 4-AA is achieved within 9 min using S1 as the photocatalyst. However, when the S2 is added to the same solution of 4-NA, there is no evidence of conversion of 4-NA to 4-AA within 9 min under visible light irradiation (Fig. 4e). To specify the activity differences of reducing 4-NP and 4-NA under visible light irradiation, the degree of the conversion is quantified by the ratio of the final concentration and the initial amount of 4-NP and 4-NA at a given irradiation time. The concentration ratio is acquired by the corresponding absorbance of typical peaks according to the Lambert−Beer law. Fig. 4f displays comparative results of conversion rates of S1 and S2 for the reduction of 4-NP and 4-NA under the visible light irradiation. S1 clearly exhibits much better photocatalytic activity in comparison with S2. Moreover, all of the above performance characterizations point to the crucial roles of CDs in mesoporous NiB nanospheres for photocatalysis.

To verify the contribution of CDs on catalytic activity, two control experiments were conducted by using different concentrations of CD solution as heterogeneous nucleation agents. We prepared sample S3 and S4 employing one-quarter and half of CD concentrations of the S1, respectively. Fig. 5 gives the activity comparisons of S1-S4 for photocatalystic reduction of 4-NP. The typical absorption peak of 4-NP decreases with the increases of CD contents in samples at the given irradiation time, accompanied by a concomitant increase of the absoption peak of 4-AP (Fig. 5a). The conversion rates of S1-S4 for the reduction of 4-NP and 4-NA under the visible light irradiation are with the above conclusions (Fig. 5b). What's more, the turn-over frequency (TOF) was calculated based on the following equation,
(1)
TOF =
A m o u n t
o f
4 ‐ N i t r o p h e n o l
( m o l )
× c o n v e r s i o n
( % )
M a s s
o f
c a t a l y s t
( g )
× r e a c t i o n
t i m e
( h )

The conversion of 4-NP has a TOF as high as 9.50 mol g−1 h−1 while the S1 is employed as the photocatalyst. By contrast, the TOF values for S4, S3 and S2 are 6.92, 5.57 and 1.86 mol g−1 h−1, respctively. Accordingly, these results clarify the key contribution of CDs made to photocatalytic activities of samples.

To shed light on what the roles of CDs play in NiB nanospheres, the band edge positions of S1 and S2 were analyzed through linear potential scans for confirming whether conduction band minimum (CBM) levels suffice to offer overpotentials for reduction of 4-NP or 4-NA [11,34,35]. The foundation for determining the band edge positions by linear potential scans is the abrupt emergence of cathodic and anodic currents when applying potentials above the CBM to form an accumulation layer, or below the valence band maximum (VBM) to form inversion layers, respectively [34,35]. Fig. 6 a and b give the cathodic and anodic scan results for S1 and S2. It can be seen that the CBM and VBM values of S1 are approximately −1.2 and 1.0 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl) while those of S2 are approximately −1.0 and 1.2 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively. The band-gap energy of both samples determined from this potential scan was 2.2 eV, implying that the incorporation of CDs into mesoporous NiB nanospheres do not alter the material band gap, but prominently adjust the band edge positions. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a reliable tool for reflecting the charge-transfer rate [12,16,19,34]. From Fig. 6c, the arc radius on the EIS Nyquist plots of S1 is much smaller than that of S2. The electron-transfer resistance of S1 on the basis of an equivalent circuit (Fig. S8, Supplementary information) is found to decrease by one-fifth of that of S2. On the other hand, the transient photocurrent responses of the S1 and S2 were measured under visible light irradiation to investigate the electrons migration properties. The current-time curves with 4 cycles of light irradiation on-off are given in Fig. 6d. Clearly, the S1 exhibits a significant enhanced photocurrent density than the S2. Hence, the EIS and photocurrent responses results illustrate that S1 has significantly higher efficiency for the separation and migration for photogenerated charges, reducing the energy-wasteful recombination. In addition, the Mott–Schottky plots of both S1 and S2 exhibit the typical n-type characteristic owing to the positive slope of the linear plots (Fig. S9, Supplementary information), implying that the electrons in favor of reduction reaction are the dominant photogenerated carriers [19,34].

Cyclic voltammetry was employed to determine the reduction potential of 4-NP, which was about −0.93 V versus Ag/AgCl (Fig. S10, Supplementary information). The band edge positions of S1 (−1.2 V) and S2 (−1.0 V) thermodynamically enable photocatalytic reduction of 4-NP. Nonetheless, the CBM of S1 is more negative than that of S2, i.e. the conduction band of S1 lies 0.2 eV above that of S2. Such band alignment, illustrated in Fig. 6e, is the driving force for the kinetics of electronic charge carriers and favors the transfer of photogenerated electrons from S1 to 4-NP, leading to S1's effective reduction ability under the visible light irradiation [36]. Comparing with solid catalysts, mesoporous structures as catalysts offer large surface accessibility and the advantage of pore confinement effect because the reactant molecules could access mesopores to induce their activation energy variations. In addition, the CDs in mesopores could act as the roles of electron reservoir [15,17] and hence promote chemical transformation of adsorbed species.

Besides reactant activation by photogenerated electrons and chemical transformation of adsorbed species, the photocatalytic reaction also involves adsorption on specific sites of a solid surface and desorption of products [3]. The incorporation of CDs in mesopores of NiB could induce a new surface charge characteristic by changing space charge distributions of NiB structure. As shown in Fig. 6f, zeta potential results demonstrate that the surface of S1 is oppositely charged to S2 while the electronegativity of 4-NP (reactant) is far larger than that of 4-AP (product). Apparently, the S1 is more liable to adsorb 4-NP than the S2. As a consequent, an adsorbed layer of 4-NP is formed on the surface of S1and the charge transfer from the S1 to 4-NP are most likely to occur under light irradiation, leading to photoreduction of 4-NP. When a 4-AP molecule is formed in the reaction front, it may be repelled by the adsorbed layer of 4-NP on the surface of S1. Therefore, the kinetic of the photocatalytic reaction in the S1 is favorable. Since the S2 is difficult to adsorb 4-NP on the basis of Fig. 6f, the charge transfer from S2 to 4-NP is limited, bad for photocatalytic reaction though it may more easily desorb the 4-AP than the S1. Consequently all of the above factors provide strong rationale for the higher activity of S1 for photoreduction of nitrobenzene derivatives.

In conclusion, the concept of incorporating CDs into mesoporous NiB hybrids presents itself a very interesting and viable option for future low-cost high-efficient photocatalysts. The method provided herein constitutes a facile rout for incorporating CDs into the mesoporous and amorphous NiB hybrids, and the results also demonstrate its impressive photoreduction activities for nitrobenzene derivatives under the visible light irradiation. In addition to promoting photogenerated charge separation and transfer in hybrid system, the CDs can also adjust the band edge positions and surface electronegativity of NiB, thus significantly improving each of physicochemical processes involved in the heterogeneous catalytic reaction. It is anticipated that this hybrid nanostructure will motivate theoretical and experimental studies to further the fundamental understanding of the roles of CDs in different nanomaterial systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125, 51272301, 51502270), Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi, North University of China Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars, and North University of China Fund for Scientific Innovation Team. We also thank our collaborator Dr Adrian Trinchi of the CSIRO Australia for his scientific discussions on this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.05.066.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: From chemistry to nanoscience: not just a matter of size
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
Hierarchically structured porous materials for energy conversion and storage
